content:
You are mistral, an autonomous AI agent powered by mistral-medium, competing in a TAIKAI hackathon. You are in the BUILD phase. Hackathon: Arena Sprint - Tic-Tac-Toe (slug: tic-tac-toe) Brief: Arena Sprint: Tic-Tac-Toe. Build, deploy, and ship a playable tic-tac-toe game that runs in the browser — built, deployed, and submitted entirely on your own. WHAT TO BUILD: a single-page web app with a working 3x3 tic-tac-toe game; two-player local play (X and O alternate) at minimum (a simple vs-computer mode is a nice bonus, not required); detect and clearly show wins, draws, and whose turn it is; include a new-game/reset. No backend or login required — keep it self-contained and fast to load. REQUIREMENTS TO BE ELIGIBLE: a public GitHub repository with the source and a clear README (what it is, how to run locally, tech used, decisions/trade-offs); the game deployed to a public URL that loads and is playable; a published TAIKAI project (name, short tagline, full HTML description) with the GitHub repo in the project Code field and the live URL in the Demo field. JUDGING: It works (40%) — the deployed game is playable and bug-free, wins/draws detected correctly; Craft (30%) — clean readable code and a clear incremental git history; Shipped (20%) — repo + live demo both reachable and linked on the project; Writeup (10%) — README and project description explain what it is and how it was built. Scope is intentionally small — favour something that works and is shipped over something ambitious but unfinished. Categories: (none declared) Judging rubric: - It works (weight 40, 0-10): Deployed game playable and bug-free - Craft (weight 30, 0-10): Clean code + incremental git history - Shipped (weight 20, 0-10): Repo + demo reachable and linked - Writeup (weight 10, 0-10): README + description explain it Operating rules (apply at all times): - Working directory is /workspace. Stay inside it. - Act by CALLING tools, not by describing actions in prose — text/markdown in your reply is never executed. Use the tool catalogue; do not invent tool names. - Work in small increments and commit after EACH meaningful step — scaffold, then each feature, then polish — with clear, descriptive messages. Do NOT save everything for one big commit at the end; a clean incremental git history is part of how you are judged. Push as you go. - Prefer the simplest thing that satisfies the goal. Optimize for working > polished. - Your GitHub repository is layerx-labs/arena-tic-tac-toe-mistral. Push commits there. - If you get stuck, call `request_nudge` with a brief reason rather than spinning. - End this phase by calling `complete_phase` — never by just saying you are done in prose. You are in the BUILD phase. Your plan from the ideation phase is saved at `/workspace/PLAN.md` — READ IT FIRST with `read_file` and build what it describes. You may refine the plan as you learn, but stay close to it. TAIKAI tools come from the live TAIKAI MCP (tool names start with `taikai_`). Your target TAIKAI challenge is org="layerx", challenge="tic-tac-toe". HOW TO ACT (read this first): - Take EVERY action by CALLING A TOOL. Writing a shell command, code, or file contents as text in your reply does nothing — text is never executed. To run a command call `shell`; to write a file call `write_file`; to deploy call `deploy_vercel`; to submit call the `taikai_*` tools. - Do NOT announce plans ("I will now deploy…") and stop. Perform the action with a tool call in the same turn. - If a tool call returns an error, READ the error and fix the cause. Do not blindly retry the same call (e.g. do not just change the project name and call again) — diagnose what the error says. Submission flow (complete in order): 1. Build the project. Commit and push to your GitHub repo as you go. 2. Deploy to a public URL — `deploy_vercel` is the default (`deploy_railway` for backends, `deploy_cloudflare` for Workers/Pages). 3. Verify the demo URL responds: `shell` with `curl -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" ` (expect 200). The sandbox has no browser. 4. Write a README in the repo: what it does, how to run, demo URL, architecture, trade-offs. 5. Resolve the challenge id: `taikai_get_challenge` with the org + challenge slugs above; save the returned `id`. (You already registered in the registration phase — do NOT register again.) 6. Get-or-create your project (you may have only ONE per challenge): a. First call `taikai_list_projects` for this challenge and look for a project whose author is YOU (your username from `taikai_get_me`). You may already have one — possibly a leftover DRAFT. b. If you already have a project, DO NOT create another (it will be rejected). Save its `id` and skip straight to step 7 to publish it. c. Otherwise create one (starts as DRAFT): `taikai_create_project` with the challengeId, a unique plain-text `name` (3-80 chars), a plain-text `teaser` (3-200 chars — a punchy one-line hook that sells what the project does and for whom; this is what judges see in the project list, so make it land, don't just restate the name), `members: null`, and `industries: null`. ⚠️ NEVER put any user id in `members` — you are the sole author; adding other participants (including ids from the participant list) is rejected. Save the returned project `id`. 7. Publish = submit: make ONE `taikai_update_project` call that includes EVERY parameter (each is required to be present — set unused ones to null): `name`, `teaser`, `description` (full HTML — see "WRITING THE PROJECT DESCRIPTION" below), `codeUrl` (your GitHub repo URL), `demoUrl` (your live deploy URL), `state: "ACTIVE"`, plus `coverImageFileId: null, logoImageFileId: null, files: null, videoUrl: null`. codeUrl, demoUrl, description and state MUST all be in this single call. `state: "ACTIVE"` is what submits it. WRITING THE PROJECT DESCRIPTION (this is your pitch to the judges — it is scored, so make it the most thorough thing you write): The `description` is the single biggest factor in how judges understand and rate your project. A one-liner loses. Write a long, structured, HTML writeup (use

section headers,

paragraphs,

    /
  • lists, for emphasis, for links) that a judge skimming dozens of submissions can grasp in seconds yet that rewards a deep read. Cover, in roughly this order: - What it is — a punchy one-paragraph summary: the problem, who it's for, and what your project does about it. Lead with the value. - How it maps to the brief — explicitly connect your project to the hackathon brief and to each judging-rubric criterion above. Name the criteria and say how you satisfy them. Judges look for this. - Key features — a bulleted list of what actually works, framed as user-facing capabilities, not internal tasks. - Tech stack & architecture — the languages, frameworks, libraries, databases, APIs, and infra you used, and WHY each was chosen. Describe the architecture (how the pieces fit: frontend / backend / data / deploy). Mention anything notable: AI models, smart contracts, integrations. Be specific and accurate — every tool you name here MUST actually appear in the repo you pushed (judges clone and read the code; a stack claim that isn't in the code is worse than omitting it). Only claim what you actually built. - How to try it — the live demo URL (as a clickable ) with any steps/credentials needed to use it, plus the code repo link. - Challenges & what you learned — real engineering trade-offs or obstacles and how you solved them. This signals depth. - What's next — a short roadmap of where you'd take it with more time. Rules: be truthful — describe only what you actually built and deployed; never invent features. Make every link a real . Aim for substance over fluff: several rich sections, not a wall of buzzwords. This description, the live demo, and the code together are what win — treat the description as seriously as the build. DEFINITION OF DONE — do NOT complete this phase until you have VERIFIED every item is true (check, don't assume): - [ ] All code is committed and pushed — run `git_status` and confirm the tree is clean. - [ ] The app is deployed and its demo URL returned HTTP 200 (you ran the curl check). - [ ] A README exists in the repo. - [ ] Call `taikai_get_project` and confirm your project has: a `name`, a `teaser` (short description), a full HTML `description` that follows "WRITING THE PROJECT DESCRIPTION" (multiple

    sections covering what it is, how it maps to the brief/rubric, features, tech stack & architecture, how to try it — not a one-liner), `codeUrl` set to your repo, `demoUrl` set to your deploy, and `state` = "ACTIVE". Only when ALL boxes are verified true, call `complete_phase` with a `summary` (project name, demo URL, repo URL, one line on what you shipped). If any check fails, fix it and re-verify. Never complete the phase based on intention — only on confirmed facts. After you complete this phase you will WAIT until every agent has submitted, then you will be asked to judge.

Your plan from the ideation phase is saved at /workspace/PLAN.md. Read it and build it.

Plan summary: Building a minimal, rock-solid tic-tac-toe web app with vanilla HTML/CSS/JS, deployed via GitHub Pages, focusing on perfect core functionality over unnecessary features.

result.content:
# Arena Sprint: Tic-Tac-Toe - Project Plan

## Project Idea
**Simple, Rock-Solid Tic-Tac-Toe Web App**

A minimalist, self-contained 3x3 tic-tac-toe game for two local players (X and O) that runs entirely in the browser. This implementation prioritizes reliability, clean code, and complete fulfillment of all hackathon requirements over unnecessary features or complexity.

## Target User & Problem
- **User**: Anyone wanting a quick, no-frills tic-tac-toe game
- **Problem**: Need a working, bug-free tic-tac-toe implementation that meets all hackathon criteria
- **Solution**: A lightweight, dependency-free web app that loads instantly and provides clear game state feedback

## Core Features (Minimum Viable Product)
1. **3x3 Game Board**: Standard tic-tac-toe grid
2. **Two-Player Local Play**: X and O alternate turns
3. **Win Detection**: All 8 possible win conditions (3 rows, 3 columns, 2 diagonals)
4. **Draw Detection**: Automatically detects when board is full with no winner
5. **Turn Indicator**: Clear visual display of current player's turn
6. **New Game/Reset**: Button to clear the board and start fresh
7. **Game State Feedback**: Visual indication of wins, draws, and current turn

## Tech Stack
- **Frontend**: HTML5 + CSS3 + Vanilla JavaScript (ES6+)
  - *Justification*: Zero dependencies = fastest load time, no build step, simplest deployment, maximum reliability
- **Deployment**: GitHub Pages
  - *Justification*: Free, integrates seamlessly with GitHub repo, automatic deployment, provides public URL
- **Version Control**: Git with GitHub (repo: layerx-labs/arena-tic-tac-toe-mistral)
  - *Justification*: Required by brief, enables incremental commit history for Craft criterion

## Architecture
### Frontend Structure
- **Single-Page Application (SPA)**: No page reloads, all game state managed in memory
- **File Structure**:
  - `index.html`: Main HTML structure
  - `style.css`: All styling (minimal, clean)
  - `script.js`: Game logic and DOM manipulation
  - `README.md`: Project documentation
- **No Backend**: Entirely client-side, no server required
- **No External Dependencies**: Pure vanilla implementation

### Game Logic Design
- **State Management**:
  - `board`: 3x3 array tracking X, O, or empty
  - `currentPlayer`: Tracks whose turn it is (X or O)
  - `gameActive`: Boolean to prevent moves after game ends
- **Win Detection**: Function checks all 8 win conditions after each move
- **Draw Detection**: Checks if board is full and no winner
- **DOM Updates**: Separate functions for rendering board state and game messages

### Deployment Strategy
1. Push code to GitHub repository
2. Enable GitHub Pages on the repository (serving from root)
3. Live URL will be: `https://layerx-labs.github.io/arena-tic-tac-toe-mistral/`

## Rubric Mapping

### It Works (40% - Weight: 4/10)
- **Implementation**: Thoroughly tested game logic with edge case handling
- **Testing Focus**:
  - All 8 win conditions for both players
  - Draw detection when board fills
  - Turn alternation enforcement
  - Reset functionality
  - Game state locking after win/draw
- **Goal**: 10/10 - Perfectly playable, zero bugs

### Craft (30% - Weight: 3/10)
- **Code Quality**:
  - Modular functions with single responsibilities
  - Clear variable and function names
  - Consistent code style
  - Inline comments for complex logic
- **Git History**:
  - Incremental commits for each feature
  - Clear commit messages (e.g., "Add win detection logic")
  - Logical progression from setup to completion
- **Goal**: 10/10 - Clean, readable, well-structured code with clear git history

### Shipped (20% - Weight: 2/10)
- **Delivery**:
  - Public GitHub repository with all source code
  - Live deployment on GitHub Pages
  - Both links properly configured in TAIKAI project
- **Verification**: All links accessible and functional
- **Goal**: 10/10 - Repo and demo both reachable and linked correctly

### Writeup (10% - Weight: 1/10)
- **README Contents**:
  - Project description and purpose
  - How to run locally (just open index.html)
  - Tech stack used and why
  - Key implementation decisions
  - Trade-offs made (e.g., simplicity over features)
  - Deployment instructions
- **TAIKAI Description**: Clear, concise project overview
- **Goal**: 10/10 - Comprehensive documentation explaining what it is and how it works

## Build Phase Milestones

### Phase 1: Project Setup (15 min)
- [ ] Initialize project directory structure
- [ ] Create GitHub repository (layerx-labs/arena-tic-tac-toe-mistral)
- [ ] Set up initial commit with README skeleton

### Phase 2: Core Structure (20 min)
- [ ] Create `index.html` with basic board structure
- [ ] Create `style.css` with minimal styling for board and cells
- [ ] Create `script.js` with empty game state initialization

### Phase 3: Game Logic (30 min)
- [ ] Implement board state management
- [ ] Implement turn alternation
- [ ] Implement move handling (cell clicks)
- [ ] Implement win detection function
- [ ] Implement draw detection

### Phase 4: UI Integration (20 min)
- [ ] Connect game logic to DOM updates
- [ ] Add turn indicator display
- [ ] Add game message display (win/draw/turn)
- [ ] Add reset button functionality

### Phase 5: Polish & Testing (25 min)
- [ ] Add visual feedback for moves
- [ ] Style game messages clearly
- [ ] Test all win conditions manually
- [ ] Test draw condition
- [ ] Test reset functionality
- [ ] Fix any discovered bugs

### Phase 6: Documentation & Deployment (20 min)
- [ ] Write comprehensive README.md
- [ ] Deploy to GitHub Pages
- [ ] Verify live URL works
- [ ] Create TAIKAI project with links

### Phase 7: Final Verification (10 min)
- [ ] Test live deployment thoroughly
- [ ] Verify all links work
- [ ] Confirm all requirements met

## Definition of Done
The project is **DONE** when:
1. ✅ Game is fully playable in browser with all core features
2. ✅ Code is clean, modular, and passes manual testing
3. ✅ GitHub repository exists with clear, incremental commit history
4. ✅ README.md is comprehensive and accurate
5. ✅ Game is deployed to GitHub Pages and accessible at public URL
6. ✅ All hackathon requirements are satisfied (two-player, win/draw detection, reset, etc.)
7. ✅ TAIKAI project is created with correct repo and demo links

## Success Metrics
- **Minimum**: All core features working, deployed, documented
- **Target**: Perfect score on all rubric criteria
- **Stretch**: Add simple computer opponent (random moves) if time permits

## Risk Mitigation
- **Risk**: Over-engineering or adding unnecessary features
  - **Mitigation**: Stick to MVP features only; focus on reliability
- **Risk**: GitHub Pages deployment issues
  - **Mitigation**: Test deployment early, use standard GitHub Pages setup
- **Risk**: Bugs in win/draw detection
  - **Mitigation**: Implement thorough manual testing of all edge cases

## Notes
- This plan prioritizes **working > polished** as per hackathon guidance
- The implementation will be intentionally minimal to ensure reliability
- No frameworks or libraries will be used to avoid dependency issues
- All code will be vanilla and self-contained for maximum portability
